We should never run in this situation. The code is actually quite confusing, but a bit of context:

- On trial checkpoint save we set `trial.saving_to = _TrackedCheckpoint(object_ref, ...)`
- But `trial.saving_to` is not serialized. It's also not tracked by the trial checkpoint manager
- Thus it will not be included in `self.get_trial_checkpoints()`
- Instead, once the future is resolved, it will be stored (as resolved) in `dir_or_data` and only _then_ be tracked form the trial checkpoint manager
- Thus, we should never run into the situation where we have an object ref here

Thus we can actually assert that this is not a object ref here, or just leave it out, or skip if it is. I'd probably prefer an assertion here

Doesn't clipping to 12am still result in problems if the notification is more than 1 day in the future?

1. it's Monday at 9am
2. notification scheduled for Wednesday at 9am
3. notification timestamp is clipped to 12am Monday
4. user successfully marks notification as read
5. Tuesday morning: notification timestamp is clipped to 12am Tuesday
6. user sees unread notification again

IMO it seems better if we fix this further downstream (not in the fetching phase) by just not showing notifications at all if they're still in the future.  That would let us use future-timestamped notifications to deliberately roll something out at a precise time if we needed that for some reason.

Or to put it another way, we'd have 3 possible states for notifications, A) in the future, B) in the past but more recent than last read timestamp, or C) in the past and older than last read timestamp.

The unread count / red dot only considers notifications in state B, and the actual notification panel only shows notifications in states B and C, so notifications in state A are entirely hidden.

> 2.) We may want to interchange between Modin implementations using this protocol (e.g. omnisci -> pandas on ray), and it is harder to do that at upper layers.

In my opinion, JSON:API spec is quite strict about what exactly pointer attribute must contain
> `pointer`: a JSON Pointer [RFC6901] to the associated entity in the request document [e.g. "/data" for a primary data object, or "/data/attributes/title" for a specific attribute].

I suggest the following:
- `initLogging` should attempt to undo both any previous `basicConfig`, by [removing all root logger handlers](https://stackoverflow.com/a/12158233), and then set `_overrideLogLevel = None`. Now we can reliably re-init by simply calling it again.
- to get test case isolation, we should create a `setUp` implementation (or `setUpClass`) including `initLogging` for all test cases that need a certain logging state. I realize this is existing code, but especially when swallowing exceptions,  it's best practice to scope the `except` blocks at tightly as possible to avoid catching exceptions that we did not intend to catch.  E.g. catching a blanket `AttributeError` or `ValueError` could easily catch all kinds of exceptions other than the ones intended.

I'd strongly recommend scoping the errors to the minimum statement required (e.g. ImportError and AttributeError to _only_ the import statements, ValueError to _only_ the `DebugDataReader` construction) and would also recommend changing `DebugDataReader` so it raises a custom exception rather than a generic ValueError for a case like this where it's expected behavior that calling code must handle.

The problem here is that everyone has their own screens. Desktop monitors usually have wider screens than laptop screens. However, even on wider screens people prefer having two or more files open side-by-side, which diminishes the width advantage of the screens. In addition, for larger monitors people increase font size to reduce eye focus exertion when reading the code. Many people also use vertical screens.

One of the extremes is 120 line length. However, even that many characters make the lines wrap when doing side-by-side review on the GitHub using 2.5k resolution, which is the most widely used display nowadays. 
Another extreme is the margin of 72 characters. This is used to be the maximum line length because old-school terminal windows have 72 characters, and punch cards had 80 characters. So, if we frequently read the code on such screens, it makes sense to follow that length of lines. Fortunately, we are not. Unfortunately, we are not aware about how others people read it. After all, we are writing not only for yourself.
I found many proofs on the Internet that people still prefer using 80 characters limit. Here are a couple of them: [Google C++ Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Line_Length) keeps that limit describing pros and cons of that decision, Linux kernel [used to enforce that limit](https://www.phoronix.com/news/Linux-Kernel-Deprecates-80-Col), but now only recommends sticking to it.

I believe there should be some reasonable tradeoff. Let's try to find it. I think the most important aspects of the line length are:
- Be able to read the code continuously without side scrolling of the screen. The horizontal/side scrolling distracts reader's attention. Especially, if the amount of side scrolling is huge. Consider doing horizontal scrolling back and forth for every line of code.
- Limiting complexity of a single line
- Limiting indentation, which in turn limiting the cyclomatic complexity by prohibiting too much of a conditional blocks, loops, and other type of  nesting.

For the 2.5K desktop monitor and FHD laptop the golden mean is ~100 characters.

I tried to use asserts in a way consistent with the blog. An assertion failure here should mean a _programming error_ since we only ever expect to call `DistributionAggregationData.__init__` from `DistributionAggregation.__init__`, where we actually do raise a `ValueError` if the user gives us unsorted bounds.

I.e. precondition checks for API methods should raise `Type`/`ValueError`s (and etc.), but precondition checks in internal-only code can assert instead since bad user input should never cause them to fail. The logic doesn't change if the assertions are removed, and they should only fail if there's a bug in the library.

That said, there's no bright line separating the API from the internals here, and we don't lose anything by changing these to `ValueErrors` (other than signaling the difference between expected and unexpected errors). If you've got a strong preference I don't mind changing it.

The error is misleading because `references[0]` is a valid call since Reference behaves like a tuple.

I would suggest to change the `opentracing.Reference` implementation to be a its own type instead, preventing this issue.

I'm glad to open a PR to opentracing-python to implement immutability without inheriting from a NamedTuple [created on-the-fly](https://github.com/opentracing/opentracing-python/blob/946f763510a8c26a66d5c53a9c4d0c6027cb9129/opentracing/tracer.py#L255).

Perhaps something like along the lines of:

```python
class Reference(object):
    """A Reference pairs a reference type with a referenced :class:`SpanContext`.
    References are used by :meth:`Tracer.start_span()` to describe the
    relationships between :class:`Span`\\ s.
    :class:`Tracer` implementations must ignore references where
    referenced_context is ``None``.  This behavior allows for simpler code when
    an inbound RPC request contains no tracing information and as a result
    :meth:`Tracer.extract()` returns ``None``::
        parent_ref = tracer.extract(opentracing.HTTP_HEADERS, request.headers)
        span = tracer.start_span(
            'operation', references=child_of(parent_ref)
        )
    See :meth:`child_of` and :meth:`follows_from` helpers for creating these
    references.
    """
    def __init__(self, type, referenced_context):
        self.type = type
        self.referenced_context = referenced_context

    def __getitem__(self, position):
        """backwards-compatibility with NamedTuple implementation"""
        if position == 0:
            return self.type
        if position == 1:
            return self.referenced_context

        raise IndexError("Reference supports only 2 positions: 0 (type) or 1 (referenced_context)")
```

I think pointer would be tricky. Here is what I would suggest:
- We cannot read the entire `LinkMetricsQueryTlv` from message, since we cannot know its size (its value can be a bunch of sub-tlvs with different lengths, and we can/should not assume some max size for it)
- So in `MleRouter::SendDataResponse()` instead of using `Tlv::FindTlv()` (which would read the entire tlv from message into some buffer), we can use `FindTlvValueOffset()` to find offset of start of value portion and its length (or can use `FindTlvOffset()` to find the offset of start of the TLV).
- We can then pass this info (the offset into start of the value portion of `LinkMetricsQueryTlv` and its length)  in the call to `AppendLinkMetricsReport()`  (note that we already pass in the `aRequestMessage`)
- The value portion of `LinkMetricsQueryTlv` is a sequence of "Thread" Link Metric sub-TLVs.
- Then in `AppendLinkMetricsReport()` we try to parse and read all sub-tlvs from the message (knowing the start offset):
   - Read the TLV from `aRequestMessage` as a raw `Tlv tlv`.
   - In a `switch`, we can check the `tlv` type and if the type is one the ones we care about, we process it further and either read its value or read it fully again from message  (example of something similar to this is [`NetworkDiagnostic::GetNextDiagTlv()`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/thread/network_diagnostic.cpp#L763))
   - For simple TLVs with single value we don't even need to define a sub-class of `Tlv` type.
   - We can use helpers `Tlv::ReadUint8Tlv` or `ReadUint16Tlv`, etc to read the value directly from the message.
   - So for example we may not even need the class `LinkMetricsQueryId` TLV, since its value is single `uint8_t` ( (series ID) and we can use the `Tlv` helper methods to read the value directly (we have similar helper methods for writing simple (single-value) TLVs into a message).
     - Something unrelated, I would highly suggest that we add a Tlv prefix to any class that is a TLV and sub-classes `Tlv` type (for example, suggest renaming `LinkMetricsQueryOptions` -> `LinkMetricsQueryOptionsTlv`)
  - For `LinkMetricsQueryOptionsTlv` (which itself can contain an array of `LinkMetricsTypeIdFlags` values, again we cannot know its size and should not assume some max size for it, so we cannot have a buffer and read it whole)
       - We should instead read its value one `TypeIdFlags`at a time and then check if the type id is one of the ones we know and if so, add them into `typeIdFlags[kMaxTypeIdFlags]` array (if not just skip it I guess).

Yeah the sdk function `sync_function_triggers` is already throwing a `DefaultErrorResponseException` so that would happen without this change anyways.

What's happening is that the sdk function `sync_function_triggers` expects the rest api call to sync triggers to return a 204. If it doesn't return a 204, it throws a `CloudError` exception. Turns out the rest api call to sync triggers  returns a 200 on success, not a 204 (not sure if this is a docs issue or what but I've tested it and it seems to be the case). So the sdk function `sync_function_triggers` throws an exception because even though it's a success, it's not 204.

Previously, this was a CloudError and we would catch that exception, and it it's a 200 status code we would ignore the exception because it was actually a success. At some point, I'm not sure when or why, the sdk function `sync_function_triggers` started throwing `DefaultErrorResponseException`s instead of `CloudError` exceptions for 200 status codes. This would result in the user seeing an exception from running config-zip and thinking it failed. So this just covers this new case of the sdk function throwing a `DefaultErrorResponseException` on success.

Value aligned as in which bit within a byte is aligned to the pin associated with the backlight.

The PCF8574 has 8 data ports (p0-p7) which are set based upon the byte that you are sending.  The bits that are set within the byte determine which data ports are set high or low.

```
dataline:         p7  p6  p5  p4 p3 p2 p1 p0
value:           128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1
```

So, for instance, if you were to send a 0x80 (128), p7 would be set high with the remaining pins set low.  Sending a 0x41 (65) would set data ports p6 and p0 high and the remainder low. 

It is completely arbitrary which data lines are connected to which signal lines on the display.  With the display that I am using, the backlight just happens to be connected to p3.  It seemed to make sense for the driver to describe the pins based upon the bitwise location as opposed to their binary value. 

So, if the PCF8574 pin that is connected to the backlight is p3, we need to 'or' an 8 with the value that we are sending to ensure that p3 is set high.

That is why the pin value is computed with the following formula:
`_pin = 1 << pin`
where `pin` is the number of the pin (starting from zero) that the backlight is connected to.

This value is then or'd with any subsequent bytes sent to the PCF8574.

We should not have to do  `returned_size - 1`, because we increment `allocated_size`, but I am still sure that we should not multiply `returned_size` to `sizeof(WCHAR)`. 

According to docs https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/winevt/nf-winevt-evtformatmessage

>BufferUsed
The size, in characters of the caller-allocated buffer that the function used or the required buffer size if the function fails with ERROR_INSUFFICIENT_BUFFER.

so the `returned_size` variable, which is given as a `BufferUsed` parameter, should be a number of characters, shouldn't it?

Considering that, we don't have to multiply indices because the type of the `buf` variable is `LPWSTR`, not just a raw bytes array. If we do multiply, we go out of bufffer's bounds. Sometimes it does not crash because `malloc` may allocate more bytes but  this is still undefined behaviour.

3) As I have mentioned above, we may control the effort to maintain the matching function by limit its functionality.

- If version is less than 1.4, we call `super(CreateView, self).__init__(element, on=on, bind=bind)`

My motivation for keeping this around was indeed to not break the anchor, especially when `certbot-auto` isn't quite fully deprecated yet and it links to it. See https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/5151e2afee98adebce061215ecda6a739c4f2ecd/certbot-auto#L1730

It currently does not affect any of our non-development installation instructions. This problem can occur when compiling cryptography/cffi and in snaps, Docker, and OS packages it is precompiled. (I suppose a user of a source based distribution like Gentoo could hit this, although not in a Python virtual environment as described in the title of this section, but I've never seen this in practice.)

This could affect whatever becomes of our `pipx` proposal, but I think this is largely a resolved problem. This used to be a very common issue (see https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1081) but since then `cryptography` and `cffi` have shipped precompiled wheels for amd64 and aarch64. Well, we need the `Item` object for both the size status check and the actual upload. While this structure means we needlessly fetch the item metadata when S3 is overloaded, it avoids more complicated conditions (e.g. first run the S3 overload check if `--status-check` is present, then fetch item metadata if the identifier is present, then check the item size if both are present, then exit successfully if `--status-check` is present), which in my opinion leads to less readable code. Alternatives are two lines with `get_item` calls, which is just as ugly, or some sort of lazy evaluation, which is somewhat complicated to implement. So I found this to be the least awkward solution.

As I understand it pivoting is used to eliminate numerical problems with gaussian elimination, but gaussian elimination isn't used in the 2x2 case and I'm fairly confident that the direct expressions here are as good as anything (I'm happy to be proved wrong). 

Furthermore, remember this is for "Simple Image Polynomial". The magic word is image, and that is presumably in a undistorted frame. The axes are presumably nearly orthogonal and that should never lead to a problem matrix. If there is an ill-conditioned matrix, someone is already seriously screwed in their model (i.e., something is already very wrong).

After grappling with this some more, I'm now all-in on a push model, as the current revision shows.  I think we need to push to the component streams in any case, as described above.  And furthermore, each component stream may require a different writing mechanism (most dramatically, single RPCs vs. streaming RPCs).  So there is nothing to be gained from trying to multiplex the split streams; instead, each one can flush itself to storage independently.

Note this also eliminates the need for `peekable_iterator`, and is generally (imho) easier to reason about.

Happy to discuss (ideally asap).  To try to restate the argument compactly: any non-buffering stream must be driven by exactly one loop, the "pump".  Anything upstream of the pump is pulled, and anything downstream is pushed.  We know that we have to pull from the logdirs, and that we have to push to the write RPCs.  So the question is at what point in between those to place the pump.  The pump must be downstream of the logdir loader (because that is all done with a pull-style multiplexer which works fine and we don't want to touch).  Also, the pump must be upstream of the split.  So I think that leaves the present updated design as the only option.

This function looks like it has gotten too complicated.  For maintainability it should be refactored into several simpler functions that are composed to achieve the same objective.  Preferred options for the standalone functions are:

1. internal private module functions not in the class:

    ```python
    def _helper_function(arg1):
        """Do some internal simple thing in this module.
    ```

2. private methods inside the same class

The goal is to keep each function short and clear while minimizing the overall size of classes.  If an internal function can be written without referencing `self` or ` cls`, it's preferable to make it a standalone private function inside the module at the end of the module.

 - TeX versions are theoretically only bux-fixes forevermore, so their versions are asymptotically approaching pi (e.g. 3.1415926)

```python
# BUILD
python_requirement(
  name='colors',

python_requirement(

)

python_requirement(
  name='tensorflow-v2',
  requirements=['tensorflow==2.0'],
  compatible_resolves=['b'],
)
```

Meaning resolves are:

* `a`: `[ansicolors, tensorflow-v1]`
* `b`: `[ansicolors, tensorflow-v2]`

We have `utils.py`, where the Tensorflow import works with both 1.0 and 2.0:

```python
# f.py
import colors
from tensorflow import some_table_api
```

```python
python_source(
  name="f",
  source="f.py",
  compatible_resolves=["a", "b"],
)
```

The dependency on `ansicolors` is fine, it's the same requirement in both resolves. But, TensorFlow is different across the two - if we matched against _every_ compatible resolve, there would still be ambiguity, even though the caller will be able to disambiguate by choosing either "a" or "b".

The implementation looks quite complex to me. I think you can simplify it by seeing this operator as a kind of `zip` operator, which is zipping the sequences and the seperators together.
Consider you expect the following input format:

``` python
seperators = [[], [7], [7]]
sequences = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]
```

Then you can calculate the 3 quantities with relative ease based on `sum` and `itertools.zip_longest`. 

``` python
concat = sum((sep + seq for sep, seq in itertools.zip_longest(seperators, sequences, fillvalue=[])), [])
segment_ids = sum(([i] * (len(sep) + len(seq)) for i, (sep, seq) in enumerate(itertools.zip_longest(seperators, sequences, fillvalue=[]))), [])
p_mask = sum(([1] * len(sep) + [0] * len(seq) for i, (sep, seq) in enumerate(itertools.zip_longest(seperators, sequences, fillvalue=[]))), [])
```

In the example above, the output is

``` python
concat = [1, 2, 7, 3, 4, 7, 5, 6]
segment_ids = [0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2]
```

I know this may seem controversial, but I really believe it's useful here for the following reasons:
- In practice, I have been working a lot with pybind11, so I am way more than a "random" user, but more of an advanced user. And still,even thought I look at the code often, I am not familiar with the code.
- In practice, the documentation was stale, and I did not got the information I wanted (which was "if I raise an std::runtime_error" what happens?), so I had to try it out and look at the code, and it took me easily 20 minutes. Documentation become obsolete, it's the way it is, and it's way more user-friendly to give some hint where to look, instead of having documentation not in sync in the code, and no way to check. This is particularly true for like enums, or this which was a sequence of `if/else if`.

I was not able to do a search easily, because I did not know what to search for (i.e. `translate_exception` and `PYBIND11_RUNTIME_EXCEPTION`). If there had been one sentence like the one I am adding, it would have taken me very little time.

Let's break this up in several steps / PRs:

1. Add the attribute as an enum.
2. Mark the enum as private. This may or may not be a build error (it should be). The enum should start with `_` and not be included in documentation because it's private.
3. Add a way to specify, in enum metadata, what values map to.
5. In nimi-python world, codegen "converter" functions for those enums. The name of the converter function should be in "expanded" metadata.
6. Add a new class "AttributeWithEnumConverter" or something like that.
7. Modify nimi-python so that the attribute, if enum, uses the converter.

how it works with pyexcel is: 

a template/source file A.jj2 can be stored in either of the following two:

1. pypi-mobans, generic template source
2. pyexcel-mobans, pyexcel specific source

.moban.yml in pyexcel would have:

targets:
  - my_local_A: A.jj2

And to make it a bit complex is:

pyexcel-mobans had a [mobanfile](https://github.com/pyexcel/pyexcel-mobans/blob/master/mobanfile.yaml), where common sections among pyexcel family are stored.

so each individual project only store their specific targets. 

In moban-update.yml case, it is mentioned in https://github.com/pyexcel/pyexcel-mobans/blob/master/mobanfile.yaml#L20 . so if your update is reviewed and committed in pypi-mobans project, moban command read .moban.yml and bring your update forward to pyexcel project.

TL;DR: no change is needed, but I think its a sensible practice to reduce what the Dockerfile interacts with to what is really needs to interact with and also update chartpress.yaml to that reduced scope. This could help with performance in the CI system or to help during local development etc, but its out of scope for this PR.

> We build the docker image on every CI run anyway, so I'm not opposed to this functionality.

`chartpress` behavior is to determine a version/tag for an image based on changes to the docker images context of influence as declared in chartpress.yaml. If this image version/tag is available already, it will pull and re-use it.

Since no dev releases are made, there will not be a already pushed image typically. Especially, if any change at all leads to a change in the image's context, as that leads to a new tag is calculated by chartpress. So, in practice, you will rebuild images all the time no matter what because of the wide image context.

If you reduce the scope of the image context so that the image is rebuilt only if something actually could have influenced it besides unpinned dependencies, it wouldn't rebuild as often etc.

I think it would be a sane choice to isolate the Dockerfile a bit, and make it only use parts of the repo instead of `COPY` the entire root of this project. But the main value of doing so is performance of the CI system in my mind, which won't be great anyhow if dev releases aren't available allowing us to re-use previous image builds.

> Why raise errors? IMO, locking is simply about blocking requests until the associated resources become free again.

Right. I was just considering the cases where double executions of the same processor with overlapping pages are submitted. That would mostly end in error, wouldn't it? Unless overwrite is already set for the next requests.

> Why make this contingent on the identity of the processor?

Because the workspace will be unlocked for the same processor requests that potentially can have different page ranges. The collisions for the same processor but different pages are handled by the METS server. However, if we get a different processor but the same pages, then the workspace is locked and that request goes into the waiting internal queue (CACHED). Consider cases when running processors cannot be done independently, i.e., the output of the first processor has to be used by the second processor. 

> IMO, if the other request concerns a disjunct set of pages, then there simply cannot be a dependency relation to the currently running processor(s)

Right, that means we must track `processor · set(pages) · fileGrp` (considering the info from the next paragraph). This becomes even harder to track (and potentially debug) what to submit directly in the RabbitMQ and what to queue internally (to cache).

> If anything, if we want to look more closely into dependencies, we should consider pairs set(pages) · fileGrp, where
    - a new request must block if its pages and input fileGrp is overlapping the locked pairs, and
    - a new job will lock its pages and output fileGrp while running
Thus, even requests on the same pages but regarding distinct fileGrps would stay independent.

Oh, right. We should also consider fileGrp in the mix of complexity...

> Not a problem: simply use the actual resolved list (or rather, set) of pages! Set operations (disjunction for adding, intersection for testing, difference for clearing) are clear and efficient.

This is of course simpler to manage although there will be more stress on the DB itself with reads/writes - which I guess is okay for now.

> So we'll now have cached and queued and running job entries. For running jobs, we agreed there is a need for some kind of timeout anyway, universally

Yes, we have some dummy timeout right now based on the submitted amount of pages (set to 200 by default multiplicated with a timeout value per page).  Which could potentially lead to timeout errors even when the output is correctly produced say due to slower processing. The amount of pages is still not part of the processing request. 

> BTW, IINM we still need to take care of atomicity in some places, i.e. use a mutex to query and change the internal queue...

Yes, there will be a race condition to the internal queue resource.

A less drastic change from the current design might be to have a single buffer in our class (doing away with `self.secondary`), with very simple locking just around pushing to it and swapping it with a local buffer in `try_request` or one of its callers.  Something like this:

```
def log(message):
    self.lock.acquire(True)
    self.buf.append(message)
    self.lock.release()

def try_request():
    self.lock.acquire(True)
    if not self.buf:
        self.lock.release()
        return

    localbuf = self.buf
    self.buf = []
    self.lock.release()

    // send the stuff in localbuf
```

Obviously this is not a functional replacement, it's just meant to demonstrate the intent -- the locking protocol is vastly simplified, we still have the ability to manage entire buffers of messages at once without any funny business, and we can continue to accumulate new messages while sending the old.  This is basically how the node agent works in fact, so it's not really a novel design even within our code base.

This logic is _very_ similar to `validate_ttl` just above.

I was going to switch and use `X-Request-Ttl` to achieve this effect. However, my understanding is that once enqueued both the body and attributes (metadata) of an SQS message are immutable.
This means that to update `X-Request-Ttl` on the message we'd have to first delete it from the queue, then re-enqueue it with the updated attributes. That would be quite a change from the way we do things today (and it feels wrong).

Because of that:
- I went with the approximate receive count
- I'm wondering if `X-Request-Ttl` is usable at all - it is if the intent is for the value to be updated by a message producer (i.e. either a gateway or the service producing the message, when re-processing the same request); it's not if the intent is for it to be updated by a daemon.

If you want to be super-strict, `\s+` is not a correct regexp, as it includes unicode whitespaces; css class names are [space separated tokens](https://www.w3.org/TR/html5/infrastructure.html#set-of-space-separated-tokens), where space characters are [more limited](https://www.w3.org/TR/html5/infrastructure.html#space-characters) than what `\s` matches. 

It is also not the same as Python's `\s` with ASCII flag on, as it doesn't include `'\v'`. 

Whitespaces defined in html spec can be found in w3lib: https://github.com/scrapy/w3lib/blob/c1a030582ec30423c40215fcd159bc951c851ed7/w3lib/html.py#L20. As parsel already depends on w3lib, it can be fine to import it from there, though required w3lib version should be bumped.

If I understand this correctly, the goal of the shunt proofs is to prove:

1. For all blocks _b_i_ in the segment proofs, _b_i+1_ is the block _k_ steps prior to _b_i_, where _k_ is whatever the back-pointer in the segment proof of _b_i_ indicated.
2. For all block _b_i_ in the segment proofs, _b_i_ is included in the same fork as _b_0_ where _b_0_ is the chain tip.

If that's true, is proving (1) strictly necessary? The fact that the MARF commits these back-pointers to blockhashes means that the referenced block is already committed to, regardless of how many steps back it was, right?

In either case, this is just a normal Merkle skip-list proof, right, with the difference being that rather than _just_ proving

_b_0_ -> _b_genesis_

We want to generate a proof that

_b_0_ -> _b_1_ -> _b_2_ -> ... -> _b_genesis

And the skip-listiness of the proof should _already_ indicate how many blocks separate each entry, right? I may be misremembering some of the properties that skip-lists give you.

You're using `["@@NULL@@"]` as a default value to this function.  This is what pylint is complaining about.  It's not good practice in python, except in a few very rare cases.  See, e.g., [here](http://satran.in/2012/01/12/python-dangerous-default-value-as-argument.html).  Instead of this, use `None` as the default value, and inside the method body do `ignore_tokens = ["@@NULL@@"] if ignore_tokens is None else ignore_tokens`.  You could also just do `ignore_tokens = ignore_tokens or ["@@NULL@@"]`, except in this case that would make it so you are unable to specify `ignore_tokens=[]`, which is probably not what you want.

keys or values (recursively) of `inferences` contains a single quote:

```python
>>> import json
>>> inferences = {"label_vocab": "',alert('hmm'),null[0],'"}
>>> js = """inferenceCallback('{inferences}')""".format(
...     inferences=json.dumps(inferences))
>>> print(js)
inferenceCallback('{"label_vocab": "',alert('hmm'),null[0],'"}')
```

I’d expect to see simply `inferenceCallback({inferences})`, without the
quotes, such that the JS gets an object literal rather than a string
literal. If it really needs to be a string for some reason, you can
create the string literal safely by JSON-encoding again (in either
Python or JS).

> My understanding though is that kvm_ functions do not set errno, so we need to parse kvm_geterr

Actually, almost _all_ library functions do some system calls, *especially* the `kvm*` stuff.
So, after _any_ system call which failed, there will be `errno` set.

Usually library utility functions do not clear `errno` if they return an error condition themself.

This avoids inventing a dozen more library error codes if most stuff may be mapped on typical `errno` codes anyway.

Yes, there were previously three additional modules that were removed, `fake_tempfile`, `fake_filesystem_glob` and `fake_filesystem_shutil`. When mocking modules with `fake_filesystem` through `unittest.mock`, which is common in the Google unit tests, the unit tests would add mocking behavior to `tempfile` through the `fake_tempfile` module. The other two modules are simple to mock, `shutil` and `glob` by mocking their `os` imports directly. However, `tempfile` is, as you note, very complex, and so after `fake_tempfile` was removed, those unit tests ported their implementations to `fake_filesystem_unittest` which provides a complete implementation of a POSIX file system.

In the case of adding `fake_filesyste_unittest` to unit tests, I think it is very appropriate to add the `tempfile.gettempdir` directory for POSIX file system support. I also think that resetting the file system for each test case makes a lot of sense. In fact, I encourage use of the `@fake_filesystem_unittest.patchfs` decorator instead of subclassing `fake_filessytem_unittest.TestCase`.

I think that for `fake_filesystem` however, the only expectation is a clean fake filesystem, and nothing more. In fact, internally at Google, we have other file systems other than the local filesystem that we provide fakes for, that extend pyfakefs fake filesystem, and for which there are no devices (no /dev/null that was added at some point to pyfakefs) and no /tmp directory, because they don't make sense in those situations.

It is very simple to add a temporary directory when setting up a test function to a test module that imports `fake_filesystem` and mocks the imports of modules under test, so I would prefer preserving that expectation.

I may need to add documentation on usage of `fake_filesystem` with the `unittest.mock` module, and also, how to extend `fake_filesystem` for non-POSIX, Windows or MacOS file systems, that are not patching imports of the modules under test, and are rather, accessing those file systems through other APIs.

From the pep8 docs.

https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/#function-and-method-arguments

> Always use self for the first argument to instance methods.
> 
> Always use cls for the first argument to class methods.

> If a function argument’s name clashes with a reserved keyword, it is generally better to append a single trailing underscore rather than use an abbreviation or spelling corruption. Thus class_ is better than clss. (Perhaps better is to avoid such clashes by using a synonym.)

@strixcuriosus it seems to me like this approach crosses boundaries between the notions of sessions and clients, which looks like it could be confusing and error-prone. My understanding is that a session is the thing responsible for holding the authentication information and doing stuff, and the client delegates some pre-determined logical flows to the session, assuming that the session knows things about API keys and what-not.

For example:
```python
with planet.Session(auth=planet.get_auth()) as session:
    with planet.OrdersClient(base_url=a_shiny_url) as client:
        oid = asyncio.run(client.create_order(order))
```
There's a bug there, and it wouldn't show up immediately. :-/

I personally _would_ like to see the batteries-included approach with top-level things, like Requests and other libraries do, e.g. `planet.create_order` would create a default `Session` _and_ a default `Client` for you, but not doing the mixing and matching of sessions and clients.

The rule of thumb (which we also put into the [tactical manual](https://github.com/ethereum/snake-charmers-tactical-manual/blob/master/style-guide.md#function-variance)) is to always use the most abstract type for **function parameters** that qualifies the function body.

And for **function return values**, usually use the concrete type that is actually being returned.

This btw mimics the default behavior that you'd also get if you do not use type hints at all. If you don't apply any type hints, you'll be able to pass in any parameter that qualifies what is needed by the function body.

This follows the explanation in the doc, but I'll add a docstring for this.

```
Because Ray clusters can autoscale, we need to request resources before
    we can allocate them. Effectively this means we have to keep track
    of requested resources (which can trigger autoscaling). Once the requested
    resources are available to be scheduled, the control loop can acquire them.

    This separation also ensures that requests can be cancelled before they resolve
    if resources are not needed anymore.
```


The flow is thus

```
# Create resource manager
        resource_manager = ResourceManager()

        # Create resource request
        resource_request = ResourceRequest([{"CPU": 4}])

        # Pass to resource manager
        resource_manager.request_resources(resource_request)

        # Wait until ready
        while not resource_manager.has_resources_ready(resource_request):
            time.sleep(1)

        # Once ready, acquire resources
        allocated_resources = resource_manager.acquire_resources(resource_request)

        # Bind to remote task or actor
        annotated_remote_fn = allocated_resources.annotate_remote_objects(
            [remote_fn])

        # Run remote function. This will use the acquired resources
        ray.get(annotated_remote_fn.remote())

        # After using the resources, free
        resource_manager.free_resources(annotated_resources)
```

**TL;DR: I prefer dictionaries over classes for this sort of thing. :)**

I personally prefer the JSON blobs and dictionaries, to avoid the cost of JSON <--> `dict` <--> class as well as the slightly slower dot attribute lookup compared to the O(1) of a `dict` lookup. What I'm saying might be a premature optimization, but I like the parallel-ish UX of something like:

```shell
curl -X POST --u ${PL_API_KEY}: -H '<json>' <create-order-endpoint> -d @order.json
```

```shell
planet orders create --json=@order.json
```

```python
import planet

def read_json(path: str) -> str:
    with open(path, "r") as f:
        return f.read()

oid = planet.create_order(read_json("order.json"))
print(oid)
```

I feel like there might be two different use cases here, and I'm not sure how to address all the things, but there's one case where using classes for examples and stuff is super nice, but then there's the case where folks are spinning up millions of these things and pushing them at the API and walking away, and so the class-based ergonomics might not be as beneficial or valuable.

Here's an example of what I'm trying to communicate :)

```python
from __future__ import annotations
import dataclasses
import dis
import functools
import json
import timeit
import typing


@dataclasses.dataclass
class SerializableDataclass:

    @classmethod
    def fromdict(cls, data: dict) -> SerializableDataclass:
        return cls(**data)

    @classmethod
    def fromjson(cls, data: str) -> SerializableDataclass:
        return cls.fromdict(json.loads(data))

    def asjson(self) -> str:
        return json.dumps(dataclasses.asdict(self))


@dataclasses.dataclass
class Product(SerializableDataclass):
    image_ids: typing.List[str]
    item_type: str
    product_bundle: str


@dataclasses.dataclass
class Order(SerializableDataclass):
    name: str
    products: typing.List[Product]


order_json = """
    {
        "name": "test_order",
        "products": [{
            "image_ids": "3949357_1454705_2020",
            "item_type": "psorthotile",
            "product_bundle": "analytic"
        }]
    }
"""


def test_order_dict_name(order_json: str) -> str:
    order = json.loads(order_json)
    return order["name"]


def test_order_class_name(order_json: str) -> str:
    order = Order.fromjson(order_json)
    return order.name


def main():
    print(dis.dis(test_order_dict_name))
    print(dis.dis(test_order_class_name))
    timeme = functools.partial(timeit.timeit, number=10_000_000)
    dict_time = timeme(lambda: test_order_dict_name(order_json))
    cls_time = timeme(lambda: test_order_class_name(order_json))
    print(f"dict = {dict_time}")
    print(f"cls = {cls_time}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

So, the reason I turned them off is because there can be literally hundreds of these warnings, and for a long time they were hiding the one or two warnings about possible integer overflows (a far more severe problem).

* I used to spend an annoyingly large amount of my time trying to get all the "unused import" warnings to go away, despite the fact that an unused import will be ignored by the compiler and linker anyway.

* I don't use snake case in some cases because the function name contains an acronym (e.g. `ECVRF`), which I think is more descriptive.

* I don't use upper-case globals in one case (network message type constants) because they're meant to reflect the same names in an associated `enum`.  For example, `StacksMessageID::Handshake` is a global, and it corresponds to the `enum` type variant `StacksMessageType::Handshake(HandshakeData)`.

* I disable `dead_code` because a lot of the time, I'll disable methods temporarily in order to debug something.

Happy to reverse these now, but I wanted you to know that I wasn't disabling them with the intent of ignoring them outright.

I think forwarding entries are used to shorten the lookup of nested workflows. Your case are about inputs, and we do not need to forward inputs. This is because:

1. if `f` finishes and checkpointed successfully, we already have `f`'s output checkpointing file, so we do not need to check the inputs during recovery.
2. If `f` does not finish, to reconstruct the output, we have to load each of `f`'s input anyway (input1, input2). Because we always forward the result of nested workflows, `input1` & `input2`'s output can be read using a single operation each.

So I won't even pretend to know how this algorithm is working, but there a couple things I can point out (since you asked for my review). The main thing is that with dask you can't blindly add a `map_blocks` to a function that doesn't work on chunks.

1. I'm guessing `argsort` is a function that can't be map_blocks'd. It needs to look at every chunk to know how to sort them. So the benefit of dask here kind of disappears. Let me know if you want a more complete explanation of this.
2. The `compute_chunk_sizes` function (as the dask documentation says) computes the result. This means that these steps to create unique_bins are not doing things lazily as a Satpy user may expect.
3. Below you do `da.from_array(np.full(...))`. This is creating a huge numpy array and then giving it to dask to be "lazy". The numpy array operation removes any dask-ness of this operation and takes up a lot of memory. Doesn't dask have a `full` function?
4. You then write to this `statistics` dask array which may be supported by dask (maybe), but it likely doesn't perform very well as it is again not a chunk-friendly operation.

Note that this algorithm you are trying to build is inherently difficult to do in a completely dask-friendly manner. The basic operation of "max" or "min" means you have to look at many data points that may not be in the same chunk.

The "best" (maybe) way to structure this algorithm would be if you could get each bin of points to be its own dask chunk. Then run `map_blocks` (or maybe a straight numpy min) along the axes of these chunks. That way the `min` function is getting a numpy array of all the points in a bin.

This is a good question. The docs defined `hasAuthed` as _Will be true if the request had `req.authorize()` called on it._ In this case, `req.authorize` has been called, but there again, those docs were written when the `no-auth` case was handled outside of `req.authorize` so this could probably be cleaned up.

The places where `noAuth`'s value is tested are:

1) in `libraries/api/src/middleware/handle.js` where an error is thrown if the API call is not public, and `hasAuthed` is `false`
2) in `libraries/api/src/middleware/logging.js` to only retrieve `clientId` and `expires` if `hasAuthed` is true
3) in `libraries/api/src/middleware/schema.js` where an error is thrown if the API call is not public, and `hasAuthed` is `false` (this seems to be a duplication of point 1)

So pre-RFC-165, there was a simple relationship between whether a method was public, and whether it was necessary for the client's authorization credentials to be authorized: namely, if a method wasn't public (i.e. the rendered scope template resulted in a scopeExpression containing one of more scopes), then the client request had to contain hawk authentication, and be authorized against the required scopes. However, post-RFC-165, it is possible for a non-authorized client request to call a guarded API, so long as the required scopes for the API are satisfied by the scopes of the anonymous role.

So if we were to consider `hasAuthed` to mean whether the full set of scopes available to the API request have been determined, then it would still fulfill its function for 1) and 3) above. For 2) I think it is sufficient to use a `clientId` if one is returned, and the same for `expires`, and not depend on the `hasAuthed` property. We can possibly remove the check in 1 or 3 if we decide they are equivalent (although I would be a little anxious to do this directly in this PR where there is already a lot of other auth change).

FYI -- I'd like another pair of eyes on this. This was deeply confusing, and not apparent how it could be recreated using method signatures.

The issue is the two keywords `showLong...` and `slurLong...`

Previously, if `showLong...` was silent, we just allied `slurLong` and `showLong` to the same value. `slurLong` defaulted True, so we got True and True. This is why I made the change to the documentation for the keyword `showLong`--it said the default was False, but that's not what was happening.

However, if you explicitly set `showLong`, you get that value, even if it conflicts with `slurLong`. I don't think this is tested anywhere.

To get the tests to pass, I had to keep the current behavior for showLong=silent and slurLong=False, but since there is no way to represent silence (without using Optional[bool]=None), I just did `if not slurLong: showLong=False`, which means there is no way to affirmatively provide `slurLong=True` and `showLong=False`, which was theoretically possible but not tested.

Acceptable? Or do you want to insert some sort of Optional[bool]=None for showLong? Given the pretzel here and the fact the documentation was misleading and no tests are failing, this seemed fair.

From previous iteration tests, (running it on open-source python repos, adding heuristics to give better [signal stats from `--display-results` output](https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets/pull/241/commits/433b75e82347b1c806af3723ae2d8f3c70fc3827)), we added a few heuristics [1] we can add here.

We can add a UUID check when calling `is_false_positive` from `high_entropy_strings.py` and a "value by itself on a line" check to `is_false_positive_with_line_context`. The latter being when files had
```python
some_list_of_ids = [
     'frequently high entropy but not sensitive',
]
```






[1] Hacky code only used in a one-off branch to improve signal, not meant to be merged as written
```python
            rest_of_the_line = string.replace(result, '').lower().strip()
            if any(
                false_positive in rest_of_the_line
                for false_positive in (
                    'id',          # Often not of value
                    're.match',    # Regular expressions have high-entropy
                    're.compile',  # Regular expressions have high-entropy
                    '<script>',    # Often not of value
                )
            ):
                continue

            if len(result) == 36:
                UUID_RE = re.compile(
                    r'^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-4[0-9a-f]{3}-[89AB][0-9a-f]{3}-[0-9a-f]{12}$',
                    re.IGNORECASE,
                )
                if UUID_RE.match(result):
                    continue

            if is_false_positive(result, self.automaton):
                continue

            # Often times IDs are listed in a list.
            # If the secret is on a line alone by itself,
            # it is likely not to be of value
            # I went from 15% signal to 41% with this on one test corpus
            BY_ITSELF_OPTIONAL_COMMENT = re.compile(
                r'^(b)?(\'\'|""),\s*?#',
                re.IGNORECASE,
            )
            if BY_ITSELF_OPTIONAL_COMMENT.match(rest_of_the_line):
                continue
```

Hmm. Looking at https://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/authentication/#tokenauthentication, I see DRF's `TokenAuthentication` docs use the "keyword" wording as well.

So not fussed about either option.

But those docs make me think of a nice alternative too:

> **Note**: If you want to use a different keyword in the header, such as `Bearer`, simply subclass `TokenAuthentication` and set the `keyword` class variable.

So we could allow doing this…

```python
from rest_framework_api_key.permissions import BaseHasAPIKey, KeyParser

class BearerKeyParser(KeyParser):
    keyword = "Bearer"


class HasAPIKey(BaseHasAPIKey):
    key_parser = BearerKeyParser()

```

The nice thing about this would be that it'd allow people to do more complicated things if need be (eg multiple perm classes with different schemes and what not), as opposed to the setting option with only allows overriding the behavior once and for all (requiring you to customize everything if you need more complex behavior).

Update, in fact there is a discussion to do here.

Initially, the `advanced` pipeline on Azure serves two independant purpose:
1) nightly builds of master
2) pre-check release for the release branches

It was ok for Windows specifically because there were not deployment to do in the two situations.

On Linux however, the deployment of the snaps to edge must occur for 1), but not for 2).

There are several possibilities to fix the problem here:
a. we can split the pipeline in two, `nightly` for 1), `advanced` for 2)
b. we can consider that 2) is not needed, because the complete tests are already done for release tags, and so leave only a `nightly` for 1)
c. we can move the trigger for release branches in the `advanced-test` pipeline, that serves the same purpose (minus the Mattermost notification, that could either be dropped or added to `advanced-test` and disabled conditionnally for test branches).

My preference goes for b., if it does not have any impact to the actual release logic, since it removes complexity instead of adding anything. As an alternative, a. is the second best since stages are highly reusable with minimal added complexity.

What do you think?

My main concern with this approach is that if we are just rendering the entire page anyway, we may as well dispense with the etag and just return the page.

That said, I think that generating the etag from the page contents is definitely the most definitive measure.

At this point, we should take a step back and think about what we are aiming to achieve by the caching of the HTML pages here.

* By caching HTML pages, we reduce server load in two ways:
1. Once a page has been generated, for the next _CACHE_TIMEOUT_ seconds the server can respond with the cached page
2. Once a client has received a page, for the next _CACHE_TIMEOUT_ seconds the client will access the cached copy from its browser cache. When the cache has expired, then an etag can be used. With this solution, the etag no longer saves any server resources.

* We have deliberately set the _CACHE_TIMEOUT_ for these pages to a very low value (15s), this means that most of the value is coming from the server side caching, and the etag. The reason for this low value is to allow for changes in the state used to render the page to propagate quickly to clients (i.e. if the content cache key or theme changes).

We could probably recover some of the benefit of the etag here, by caching the etag value (I know this sounds a little bit crazy) - but this would probably only help if we did a pre-emptive recalculation strategy. i.e. if the etag is requested, but the cache timeout on the etag has not expired yet (but may expire soon) then we would kick off a background recalculation of the etag, rather than do it synchronously.

> what are the relevant codegen rules in that case?

The way the code currently works is that when it encounters a `SourcesField` subclass, we first check if it's a direct subclass of one of the `for_sources_types`. So if you have `for_sources_types=(PythonSourceField,)` and then a `PythonTestSourceField` instance, that's easy. Then, if that fails, we inspect the `UnionMembership` to see if there is a codegen rule that goes from the instance -> one of the expected types.

The issue is that we error if there are >1 rules. I'm proposing that we remove that restriction. If the sources instance can be converted into >1 of the expected types, cool. Generate it all.

Although, I'm not certain still that's a good change. If we set `for_sources_types=(SourcesField,), enable_codegen=True` and you have Protobuf sources, that would result in including the original `.proto` files _and_ generating Python _and_ generating any other languages like Go and Java. The `.proto` files are a subclass of `SourcesField`, and the languages can be generated into subclasses of `SourcesField`.

The answer, at least for master, is "sort of". (The direction I will be pushing after this release is "no").

The python function definition is hashed. If you change the function definition then you'll change the hash.

If you change some probably irrelevant parts of the function definition you will change the hash (such as line number) - for example, adding a one line #comment at the top of your source file invalidates all checkpoints, because it increments the line number for each function by 1.

If you change a helper function that you're calling from the function definition, you won't change the hash.

I intend this to change by 1.1 because this is confusing.

A short honest summary is "changing the source code of your workflow/program will sometimes invalidate checkpoints and sometimes not".

This unfortunately won't work because it relies on a private variable that's not always present. This change is a fundamental rework of how we compute this which is breaking. Objects that currently implement `read` without `_size` will stop emitting a 100. This also introduces a forward compatibility risk, that if an object ever removes, renames, or changes data types of the private `_size` variable, this logic fails unexpectedly.

If we introduce a solution, we need it to rely solely on public interfaces. It also needs to operate without reading the body, because some inputs are not rewindable and the data will be lost. This is what introduces the complexity that have prevented previous fixes.

Yeah, it's really not that obvious. Basically, if you have a thread with coroutines, you have concurrency in that thread between coroutines so you need a gevent-lock to make sure they are excluded and scheduled correctly.
The coroutine also needs to exclude threads, so you need a thread-lock to make sure you have, in the end, one thread and one coroutine acting.

I think what I was scared of is that you can use a gevent-lock from 2 threads, but it seems to work fine if you use a thread-lock first to synchronize. Scary!

```python
import threading
import sys
import gevent.monkey
import time

gevent.monkey.patch_all()

gl = threading.Lock()

sleep = gevent.monkey.get_original("time", "sleep")
thread = gevent.monkey.get_original("_thread", "start_new_thread")
tl = gevent.monkey.get_original("threading", "Lock")()


def lock_unlock():
    while True:
        tl.acquire()
        gl.acquire()
        print("thread got it")
        sleep(0.01)
        tl.release()
        gl.release()
        sleep(0.01)


th = thread(lock_unlock, ())

while True:
    tl.acquire()
    gl.acquire()
    print("gevent got it")
    time.sleep(0.01)
    tl.release()
    gl.release()
    sleep(0.01)
```

This will sleep for *any* reload, even if it's from cleanup. Perhaps that is not necessary.

What gives me pause is how the nginx master will behave when it receives a `SIGHUP` when it is already in the middle of reloading the configuration. As far as I can understand the nginx code, (at most) one reload will end up queued behind one that is already running.

If there are back-to-back nginx challenges and choose not to sleep after the cleanup, then we may end up in the situation where we actually have to wait for between 1.0-2.0x the duration of `--nginx-sleep-interval`, since the original reload (from the cleanup restart) has not finished yet. There also might be a filesystem race, depending whether nginx reads config files lazily or not during parsing.

Because of those concerns, I have implemented this in a conservative way (always sleep for any reload), but it comes at the cost of speed.

This is a tricky one.

The `scopes` input parameter of `_get_token(...)` function is a tuple:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/b6c8e436888f93bee8a1e4b45700671e754b8500/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/authentication.py#L40

If the `scopes` argument has multiple items, the original implementation had a bug here:
`token = self._credential.get_token(scopes)`
Because the tuple is not unpacked, the `_credential.get_token(...)` function received a tuple of tuples, for example, here:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/aff33bd40c12b16ec3eeaf9ccb7925f884a8a913/sdk/identity/azure-identity/azure/identity/_internal/msal_credentials.py#L161
Therefore, during the token acquiring process, the "scope" was the appended string of the elements in the tuple which is of course wrong, the authentication failed.

So, I changed the line to:
`token = self._credential.get_token(*scopes)`

This solved the original problem but led to a different one. The `_create_scopes` function returned a simple string in which the unpack operation resulted a tuple of characters. This, of course, does not work either.

So, I changed the `_create_scopes` function to return a tuple with one element, the `,` after `scope` does this.

I believe this change is necessary to resolve the original bug (independently from the original purpose of this PR), however, I am happy to either add a short comment why it is done so, or moving the tuple creation out of the `_create_scopes` function to the `_get_token` function.

For the multiple callable classes case (difference callable classes, or same callable class with different construction args), we can't support this since we can't initialize multiple callable classes in the actor constructor. Callable classes are initialized once, in the actor constructor when an actor worker is created, and we can only initialize one callable class at a time since we need to provide `fn_constructor_args` and `fn_constructor_kwargs` as flat arguments so object references get properly resolved in the actor creation task.

For the normal function + callable class case, this is actually supposed to be supported (`fuse()` is designed to support it), so I think that this fuse check is too strict. Let me fix that!

This will fail if the keywords change type - and will let your index in a strange state. Assume "oldSet" to be `OOSet(['a', 'b']), "newSet" to be `OOSet([1, 2])`. Then under Python3, the `difference` will result in a `TypeError` which lets your document remain indexed under `oldSet` and gets newly indexed under `missing`.

Under Python 3, all indexed values must have a common type. Changing the keyword type will therefore not work (apart from constructed cases). Therefore, you should let an exception from the `difference` calls propagate (maybe log and provide a more specific error message) and not turn it into `missing`.

This is something that I had to do to allow for a major version != 0 or 1. Without this, a dependent module would not be able to specify say: 
```
require(
    github.com/dask/dask-kubernetes v2023.3.2
) 
```
as this would fail with `version "v2.5.10" invalid: should be v0 or v1, not v2023 syntax`. After finding https://github.com/golang/go/issues/35732 and reading the associated [docs](https://go.dev/doc/modules/release-workflow#breaking), I've realized that each breaking change (for >=2.0.0) is realized in the form of a new module, postfixed with the major version (as here). 

I'm not a particular fan of this given that the `dask` ecosystem uses CalVer, but `go` is built around SemVer, so I don't think there's much we can do if we want to have things in the same repo. It also means that we'll have to increase this major version each year, which doesn't seem ideal.

Happy to reconsider whether we should move the `go` code here in case you have concerns

I think with this solution a problem comes up when you specify a wheel commit to test against (e.g. for bisecting). E.g. to test the current version of a release test (on the Ray master repo) against past wheels. This would then check out a different version of the release test (possibly non-existing)

The `build_pipeline.py` checks out both the ray test repo/branch and the ray wheels repo/branch to find the respective latest commit. This happens in the course of a few seconds. So yes, this can be outdated, but it's much closer in time than what we currently have in different jobs. 

Generally I think it this way: The build_pipeline step should take full control over what is being run in the subsequent job. It resolves the wheels we want to test on, and it resolved the test repo+branch we want to run on. Moving this resolving to the run_release_test.sh makes it a bit complicated.

We have:

```
  git clone --depth 1 -b "${RAY_TEST_BRANCH}" "${RAY_TEST_REPO}" "${TMPDIR}"
```

in that script, I think checking out a possibly set `RAY_TEST_COMMIT` is a clean solution for the script and can be easily set by the pipeline builder. What do you think?

The point of the `**kwargs` passthrough is to make this friendly to current and future `.map_batches()` arguments, where subclasses of `Preprocessor` can opt in to passing any of these args, including custom preprocessors. Otherwise, we'll need update the base `Preprocessor` whenever exposing a new parameter, and devs/advanced users will either be blocked from using that parameter until the next release or will have to override `Preprocessor._transform()`. Explicitly enumerating each potential field that may or may not exist on the preprocessor will also be more complex than this kwarg passthrough.

>where ** is used for ray_remote_args that ideally we don't want to be mixed with param values above.

What if we (or a user) has a preprocessor that we (they) want to run on a GPU, or it's mostly I/O-bound so we (they) to request a fractional CPU, or we (they) want to specify custom retry logic? We _do_ want to expose these.

Since this kwarg passthrough isn't exposed to any users except for advanced users implementing custom preprocessors, is this really an issue? I feel like the future-proofing + simplicity advantages outweigh the disadvantages.

Actually, this requires a deeper dive. And it's one that I didn't recognize (mostly) until now. I'm attaching a screenshot of the documents currently available for help in dissecting this though. When we say something is published, we don't mean is it available or published online. We ask is it precedential (having the authority to bind lower courts). Not all opinions carry the same weight or are binding. The easiest way to tell if someone is precedential here is if it gets "reported" in an official reporter. In this case, I believe the official reporter is the Military Journal (MJ). You can have a west, lexis, neutral and MJ citation but the MJ citation is basically what denotes that it is Published and precedential. In other scrapers, you could assign pretty much all full opinions as published if its a Supreme Court for example. But even there we wouldn't classify orders and minor documents precedential. So for the Coast Guard, I think you can check if the document has an MJ citation and also that it is not an opinion. If those two parts are satisfied, set it as precedential (`Published`). If it has an MJ citation but is an order, I would hedge and mark it as `Unknown`, and otherwise I would say non-precedential (`Unpublished`)

> How do I write my own nested type to use here?

The idea was to follow our documented pattern for inputs:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/afe109621e878aaf717044bf9c7f0958c53f6ffa/sdk/go/pulumi/types.go#L767-L829

But it is an unfortunate amount of boilerplate to have to write manually (especially so if you'd need to also manually write associated boilerplate for array variants, map variants, etc.), so I fully empathize with the desire from (2) to use the types that the Go SDK codegen generates. However, ideally, we'd have a way to write a much simpler type in the implementation without all the extra ceremony/boilerplate, and drive the schema + SDK generation based on it, not the other way around.

> However, the types implemented in the SDK don't seem to work correctly with the logic as implemented here.

Hmmm, do you happen to recall the specific issue? I believe there is a bug in the current implementation in this PR, where the unmarshalled value from the gRPC call isn't converted into the struct type -- so it'll remain a map. I am addressing this issue with the changes for prompt values.

> It's also a little strange that these accept Input types - given that at least currently they will always be Outputs, and even once prompt values are possible, the user will always want to be clear here in the types which ones are prompt and which are Output. It will never be desirable for the implementor to see Input types vs. Output types. The implementation will always have to immediately turn the inputs back into outputs to work with them.
>
> If this args struct used Outputs, the implementation here would also be much simpler and less "weird" in that it wouldn't have to discover To<>OutputWithContext to figure out what the output type was to create.

This is a good point. I was thinking you'd write the component implementation the same way you'd write it if you were releasing it as a regular Go library, where (ignoring prompt values for a moment) you would want to type inputs as the `Input` type to support either `Input` or `Output` values (and inside the implementation you'd immediately convert to `Output` to work with it).

But for the implementation of multi-lang components in Go, I could see how typing it as `Output` would simplify things here. We'd immediately know the `Output` type to use and it would save the user from having to convert the value to `Output` inside the component implementation.

I do still feel like we could support `Input` though... I could see supporting either `Output` or `Input`. If it's `Output`, great -- use it. If it's `Input`, one thing I could do is get its `ElementType()` and then see if the output type has been registered with `pulumi.RegisterOutputType` (using it if so), and finally falling back to looking for a `To<>Output` method.

> discover To<>OutputWithContext

Note: after the recent discussion of possibly deprecating the `<>WithContext` methods, I've switched this to look for just `To<>Output`.

Here we are trying to construct sets from the iterables exposed by `GetAce()` that contains tupple. This way, we can leverage the comparison feature of sets, and of tupple, to quickly assert about the equality of everything here, using the appropriate equality operation of each object.

Note that ACE can be defined with three or five elements.

However, in tuple the equal operation can be transfered in each member if these members are hashable. But the last element (third of fith), that is the SID on which the ACE applies, is not hashable. So I convert them in string that are hashable. The string conversion on a PySID is safe because it uses internally `win32security.ConvertSidToStringSid`.
